coredata.dc:title: Ultra-low level detection of biomarker bilirubin by graphene quantum dots and bovine serum albumin enabled turn-on sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Karmakar, Sudip
Kanti Das, Tushar
Saha, Abhijit
coredata.dc:description: It is known that concentration of unbound (free) bilirubin (BR) instead of the total bilirubin is the critical determinant of cellular uptake and toxicity of bilirubin. Thus, developing fast, effective and sensitive technique for detecting BR, a unique and important biomarker of liver dysfunction, has a long-standing demand. In this study, attempts were made to explore if biocompatible graphene quantum dots (GQDs) could be employed to probe BR at an ultra-low level in aqueous environment through optical techniques. Thus, understanding the possible interactions involved between GQDs and BR is important and necessary to develop protocol for probing BR. The detailed spectral analysis shows that BR molecule quenches the fluorescence of GQDs which may be attributed to a possible fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) involved between the excited state of GQDs and BR. The micro-calorimetric and circular dichroic (CD) measurements have provided binding and structural information in the process of interactions. It is established that with the help of synthesized GQDs, BR can be detected as low as 0.15 nM selectively and this appears to be the most sensitive amongst other available methods. Interestingly, it is observed that the addition of bovine serum albumin (BSA) facilitates the recovery of the BR-induced loss in fluorescence intensity of GQDs. The adopted strategy demonstrates that GQD may act as a sensitive fluorescent “turn off–on” probe for detection of BR. The proposed optical method based protocol validated with serum samples isolated from blood for rapid detection of BR at ultra-low level may help in developing a suitable sensing device in future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
Bilirubin
Turn-off–on sensing
BSA
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Collection of serum sample and measurement of BR
Selectivity of binding of BR to GQDs
Applicability of GQDs in determining BR of serum samples

T.K.D. is grateful to Council of Scientific and Industrial Research for the NET fellowship. The authors wish to acknowledge Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Kolkata for providing with the CD measurement facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra of GQDs and bilirubin.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of GQDs in the presence of BR in the concentration range of 0–300 nM; Inset: Stern-Volmer plot of the fluorescence quenching of GQDs; (b) FRET based emission quenching of GQDs and enhancement in intensity of BR in the concentration range of 0–200 µM; Inset: Normalized absorption and fluorescence spectra of GQDs.

Recovery of emission intensity of GQDs quenched in the presence of BR (200 µM) with gradual addition of BSA to the mixed solution.The intensity quenched due to 200 µM BR has been taken as control value (Io).
FTIR spectra of (a) GQD, (b) BR added GQD, and Inset: BSA added to the mixture of GQDs and BR.
CD spectra of GQDs-BSA in the absence and in the presence of 5 or 10 μM BR.
Fluorescence intensities of GQDs in the absence and in the presence of different substances.
Determination of BR in human serum sample.

head.ce:title: Ultra-low level detection of biomarker bilirubin by graphene quantum dots and bovine serum albumin enabled turn-on sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Kolkata Centre, III/LB-8 Bidhannagar, Kolkata 700 106, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Kolkata Centre, III/LB-8 Bidhannagar, Kolkata 700 106, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is known that concentration of unbound (free) bilirubin (BR) instead of the total bilirubin is the critical determinant of cellular uptake and toxicity of bilirubin. Thus, developing fast, effective and sensitive technique for detecting BR, a unique and important biomarker of liver dysfunction, has a long-standing demand. In this study, attempts were made to explore if biocompatible graphene quantum dots (GQDs) could be employed to probe BR at an ultra-low level in aqueous environment through optical techniques. Thus, understanding the possible interactions involved between GQDs and BR is important and necessary to develop protocol for probing BR. The detailed spectral analysis shows that BR molecule quenches the fluorescence of GQDs which may be attributed to a possible fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) involved between the excited state of GQDs and BR. The micro-calorimetric and circular dichroic (CD) measurements have provided binding and structural information in the process of interactions. It is established that with the help of synthesized GQDs, BR can be detected as low as 0.15 nM selectively and this appears to be the most sensitive amongst other available methods. Interestingly, it is observed that the addition of bovine serum albumin (BSA) facilitates the recovery of the BR-induced loss in fluorescence intensity of GQDs. The adopted strategy demonstrates that GQD may act as a sensitive fluorescent “turn off–on” probe for detection of BR. The proposed optical method based protocol validated with serum samples isolated from blood for rapid detection of BR at ultra-low level may help in developing a suitable sensing device in future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD), a 0-D carbon-based material, consists of a few layers of graphene having size less than 20 nm [1]. Functional groups like –COOH, –OH, CO are attached to the basal plane and edges of its structure. GQD is being studied for the past few years due to its unique properties, like strong PL emission, good aqueous solubility, biocompatibility and so on [2,3]. Cadmium and other heavy metal based semiconductor nanoparticles (CdSe, CdTe, HgS, etc.) have limitations in in-vivo applications due to high cytotoxicity [4]. In contrast, GQD consisting of primarily carbon, oxygen, hydrogen, nitrogen is considered more environmentally friendly and non-toxic or of very low toxicity which enable this material to use in different biological applications, like biosensing, drug delivery, etc. [5–7].

Fluorescent sensors have emerged as a promising class of sensors for detecting different analytes owing to its sensitivity, cost-effectiveness and simplicity of straight-forward measurement based on optical methods [8,9]. The photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs can be strongly impacted by alterations in the surrounding environment because of their tiny size and high sensitivity. As a result, in recent years, GQD has gained popularity as a promising candidate for fluorescence switches. The two basic categories of fluorescent switches are “turn-off” and “turn-off–on.” Fluorescence of GQDs may get quenched on interactions with different molecular or ionic species resulting in the formation of “turn-off/switch-off” sensors [10,11]. GQDs based various fluorescence turn-off sensors were developed to detect various ions [12,13], biomolecules [14,15], etc. There are several reports on fluorescence turn-off sensors based on GQDs. Amjadi et al. introduced a turn-off fluorescence probe employing glucose derived GQDs for Au (III) ion in water and plasma with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.5 µM [16]. Recently, it has been shown that red light emitting GQDs synthesized from guava extract can act as ‘turn-off’ probe for selective detection of toxic Hg2+ ions with LOD of about 80 µM [17]. Li et al. developed histidine- and pentaethylenehexamine-functionalized GQDs (PEHA-GQD-His) as the fluorescence probe for microRNA fluorescence biosensing nanoplatform coupled with molecular beacon double cycle amplification strategy. They claimed that this strategy had enabled to detect microRNA-141 with the detection limit of 4.3 × 10−19 M [18].

On the other hand, the category of “turn-off-on” fluorescent switches involving GQDs is relatively less compared to the “turn-off” switches. In “turn-off-on” switches, an additional fluorescence recovery process is required following fluorescence quenching. There are few reports where PL emission intensity of GQDs initially quenches in the presence of certain analytes (ions/molecules) and subsequent recovery of emission on addition of some specific molecules which may have the ability to remove or dissociate the quenching species from the surfaces of GQDs. For example, Senol et al. reported fluorescent “Turn off–on” sensor, based on P, N-doped graphene quantum dots which they used to sense Hg2+ and cysteine [19]. This is similar to what was reported with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) acting as turn-off/on sensor to detect Hg2+ (turn off) followed by recovery of fluorescence by S2− (turn on) [20]. The adopted approach takes advantage of the stronger binding affinity between analytes (cations/molecules) and added ions/molecules compared to that between analytes and GQDs, facilitating the restoration of quenched fluorescence emission [21]. Turn off–on sensors have advantages over turn-off sensors due to the recovery of the fluorophore intensity suggesting possibility of reuse of the probe [22]. Therefore, it is of great importance and presents a challenge to explore suitable and effective strategies for fluorescence recovery in the development of “turn-off-on” fluorescent switches.

Thus, in the present work, it has been endeavored to look into possibility of use of excitation independent GQDs synthesized through our method [23] as a fluorescence ‘turn-off/on’ probe for detection of free bilirubin which is a key biomarker of jaundice disease in human [24,25]. An enhanced concentration of BR in blood serum is observed due to liver dysfunction, biliary obstruction or hemolyticanemia [26]. Therefore, it is very important to determine the level of free bilirubin in blood precisely. The normal concentration of BR in our blood lies between 7–25 µM [27,28]. Sensing of BR in human blood serum is an important aspect as the level of BR in the blood serum provides important information about the underlying disease conditions. The conventional method, like diazo method and vanadate oxidase methods for determination of BR are time consuming and involves multistep processes [27,29]. Recently, there are some reports on determination of BR through electrochemical method, fibre optics sensor, chromatographic and electrophoresis method [30–33]. Most of them requires either costly instrumental setup or involves cumbersome procedures. One of our recent works demonstrated that BR from the serum sample can be determined employing spectroscopic technique using bovine serum albumin (BSA) encapsulated CdSe QDs [34]. Determination of analytes through spectroscopic route has several advantages over other methods [35]. It is well known that BR has a very strong binding affinity to BSA and this has spurred our interest to study the interactions of the BR with GQDs in the presence of BSA. It is shown here that BR can bind to BSA even when it is associated GQDs. Thus, the present work demonstrates a control strategy for designing a “turn off-on“ fluorescence switch for sensing BR by employing GQDs and BSA.

Natural graphite powder (300 mesh) was obtained from Alfa Aesar, USA. Perchloric acid (70 % v/v), was obtained from Merck, India. Bilirubin (AR grade) and Bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratory, India and Sigma Aldrich, USA, respectively. GQDs were synthesized following the procedure reported in our earlier work [23] as mentioned briefly in the supplementary.

Absorption spectra were recorded with PerkinElmer Lambda 950 UV/VIS/NIR spectrophotometer, employing a quartz cuvette with a path length of 10 mm, at room temperature. Fluorescence spectra of GQDs were monitored using a Luminescence Spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer Instrument LS 55). The emission lifetime of GQDs was measured with Fluorolog Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) spectrometer (Horiba Scientific) using a Delta Diode (DD-280) nanosecond laser. IBH DAS6 software was utilized for fluorescence decay analysis, taking consideration of reduced chi-square value. GQDs were excited at 285 nm and emission was monitored at 440 nm. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired using a Perkin Elmer FT-IR/FIR Spectrometer (Frontier) with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and a scan range of 8500–350 cm−1. FTIR measurements of liquid samples were carried out with ZnSe window.

Circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy was performed on a Jasco J-815 spectropolarimeter equipped with a xenon lamp and calibrated with (+)-D-10-camphor sulfonic acid. The experiment was conducted at a constant temperature of 25 °C, using a cuvette with an optical path length of 1 mm. Spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 190–250 nm at a scan rate of 50 nm/min. An isothermal titration calorimeter (ITC200, Microcal, Northampton, MA) with a normal cell volume of 200 μL was used to measure the energetics involved in the interactions of GQDs and BR. The experiment was carried out at 25 °C. The thermodynamic parameters were obtained from commercial ORIGIN software after a simple fit of the data to a single site binding model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection of serum sample and measurement of BR
ce:section.ce:para: Blood sample (2 mL) was collected and centrifuged for 15 min at 2000 rpm in a refrigerated centrifuge. The supernatant plasma was carefully separated and stored according to the method described by Tuck et al [36]. 100 μL of serum were mixed with 600 μL of GQDs. Different concentrations of BR from a stock solution were spiked into the mixture in such a way that final concentration of BR ranges in micromolar level [34]. The BR-induced luminescence quenching of the GQDs was monitored and a calibration curve was plotted. The added and obtained concentrations of BR are used to determine the % recovery in the process.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs and BR have been shown in Fig. 1 . A broad absorption band around 330 nm has been observed in case of GQDs, whereas, bilirubin has an absorption band around 450 nm. Thus, it appears that absorption of BR has no significant overlap or interference near the absorption maximum of GQDs.

GQDs were subjected to excitation at 320 nm, with fluorescence emission recorded at 440 nm. The change in the emission of GQDs was observed upon the incremental introduction of a BR solution within the concentration range of 0–300 nM. The addition of BR to GQDs resulted in a quenching of emission intensity of GQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . The quenching phenomenon exhibited a linear relationship in accordance with the Stern-Volmer relation (eqn. (1)).
(1)
I 0
/ I = 1 +
K
SV
Q
where, I0 and I are the PL emission intensities of GQDs in the absence and in the presence of BR, respectively. [Q] is the added molar concentration of BR in the solution of GQDs and Ksv is termed as the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. Remarkably, a notable reduction in the fluorescence emission of GQDs was observed even at an exceedingly low concentration of 5 nM of BR. The determination of the Stern-Volmer constant yielded a value of 1.43 × 106 M−1. This high Ksv value serves as an indication of a strong interaction between GQDs and BR.

The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by using the relation LOD = 3σ/S, where, σ represents the standard deviation and S stands for slope of the calibration curve (Fig. 2a, inset). LOD of bilirubin in the present method with the synthesized GQDs was calculated to be 0.15 nM which is far below than the normal concentration (7–25 µM) of BR in blood [27]. In this context, a comparison on the performance of detecting BR with other carbon quantum dots through fluorescence based methods is presented in Table S1. The strong quenching of the fluorescence emission of GQDs in the presence of BR even in the concentration range of 0–10 nM may be explained in terms of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. In the case of GQDs and BR, the fluorescence emission of the GQDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of BR within the 410–570 nm range (as shown in Fig. 2b, inset). It is observed that GQDs emit in the range of 360–550 nm with an emission maximum at 440 nm when excited at 320 nm. Thus, an energy transfer may occur from the excited state of GQDs to the ground state of BR, resulting in the excitation of BR which leads to an increase in emission around 550 nm (Fig. 2b) at which free BR emits. As the concentration of BR increases, the emission intensity of GQDs is quenched, while the emission intensity of BR is enhanced suggesting involvement of FRET. The small size of GQDs and strong interactive forces allow for its close proximity with BR, enabling an efficient FRET process to take place. BR possesses hydrophilic groups that may interact with GQDs, potentially forming hydrogen bonds that bring the molecules into close proximity facilitating FRET process [37]. This could be similar to what was assumed as the FRET quenching process operated between nitrogen doped carbon dots and BR [11].

It is well-known fact that affinity of BSA towards bilirubin is one of strongest possible binding in biological systems and this has led to monitor the possible effects on spectral features due to addition of BSA in a mixture of GQDs and BR [38]. A solution of BSA (0.45 mM) when added gradually to the solution of GQDs (2 mg/mL) whose emission was quenched in the presence of BR (200 µM), led to a recovery in the fluorescence emission of GQDs. Fig. S1 presents the recovered fluorescence spectra of quenched GQDs with increasing BSA concentrations (0–0.36 mM). It was observed that with increasing BSA concentration, there was simultaneous gradual enhancement in emission maxima around 440 nm and 525 nm, for GQDs and BR, respectively.

The binding of BR to BSA likely involves multiple non-covalent interactions alongside conformational changes in both molecules which was later confirmed by circular dichroism spectroscopy. Hydrogen bonding is a significant contributor, with carboxyl groups and pyrrole nitrogen atoms of BR forming hydrogen bonds with amino acid residues on BSA [39]. In addition, electrostatic interactions between the negatively charged carboxyl groups of BR and positively charged regions on BSA may further stabilize the complex. Hydrophobic interactions play a crucial role in the stabilization of the BR-BSA complex. Affinity labeling studies established that the primary binding site of bilirubin is located at residues 124 to 297 and, to a lesser extent, at residues 446 to 547 of BSA [24]. The hydrophobic side chains of BR interact with nonpolar regions on BSA, which may reduce the solvent exposure of hydrophobic regions of BR, leading to a more stable complex [37]. Thus, the addition BSA to the GQD-BR mixture might lead to competitive binding interactions, displacing BR molecules from the BR bound GQDs. This may presumably be attributed to greater binding affinity of BSA towards BR compared to that of GQDs to BR. These displaced BR molecules would then contribute to the free BR signal around 525 nm. The addition of ca. 0.36 mM of BSA enabled to recover the quenched fluorescence of GQDs by about 65 % as shown in Fig. 3 . Thus, BSA can switch on the emission of GQDs when quenched with BR and this faciltates further use of recovered GQDs for BR sensing.

The lifetimes of GQDs were measured using a 280 nm laser as an excitation source. The decay profiles are shown in Fig. S2. The decay profiles are fitted by using tri-exponential relation (eqn. (2)) [40].
(2)
I
t
=
A
1
e
( - t /
τ
1
)
+
A
2
e
( - t /
τ
2
)
+
A
3
e
( - t /
τ
3
)
Here, A1, A2, and A3 are amplitudes and τ1, τ2, and τ3 are the corresponding lifetime components. Average lifetime (τav) value of GQDs was determined to be 7.3 ns [41]. When BR was added into the solution of GQDs, the τav value reduced to 6.5 ns. It is known that FRET process causes decrease in fluorescence lifetime of the donor. Thus, the depletion of fluorescence of GQDs by BR molecules and decrease in its fluorescence lifetime may result from the FRET process involved as discussed above [42]. It was also observed that addition of BSA to the BR mixed solution of GQDs resulted an increase in τav to 7 ns.

Upon the addition of BR to GQDs, the first decay component, τ1, remains the dominant contributor to the average lifetime (Table S2). However, the population of the fastest decay component is significantly reduced. Subsequently, upon the addition of BSA to the mixture of GQDs and BR, there is a noticeable increase in the population of τ2, leading to a lengthening of the decay time and contributing greatly to the average lifetime of GQDs. In contrast, the decay time of the slowest component drops significantly. The interactions between BR and GQDs disturb the GQDs' structure, resulting in a decrease in the average lifetime. However, the addition of BSA allows BR to bind with it, thereby improving the structure of GQDs and resulting in an increase in the average lifetime.

In order to understand the influence of possible binding of BR and/or BSA with GQDs, FTIR spectra of GQDs in the absence and in the presence of those components were recorded as illustrated in Fig. 4 . The band at 1420 cm−1 arising from –OH bending of carboxylic acid in GQDs got shifted to 1410 cm−1 [43]. The broad band appearing near 3280 cm−1 in Fig. 4a is attributed to –OH stretching of carboxylic acid in GQDs. On the other hand the band appearing at 3400 cm−1 is attributed to –NH stretching of BR.

However, when BSA was added to the mixture of GQDs and BR, the band appeared at 1405 cm−1 and band at 1638–1619 cm−1 due to the sp2 hybridized CC in GQD broadened on interactions with BR Fig. 4 (inset) [44]. A band resulting from –NH bending appeared at 1550 cm−1 when BSA was added to the mixture of GQDs and BR [45]. The band at 1115 cm−1 due to C-O stretching of carboxylic group narrowed down upon adding BSA to the mixture of GQDs and BR (Fig. 4) indicating possible ground state interactions between GQDs, BR and BSA [46]. The broadening of − OH and − NH stretches, as well as changes in − NH bending and C–O stretching frequencies, suggest that hydrogen bonding might be occurring among these groups as discussed in the quenching process above.
In order to monitor the interactions among GQDs, BR and/or BSA, CD spectra of GQDs and BSA mixture were recorded with addition of increasing concentration of BR (Fig. 5 ).

The CD spectrum of the mixture of BSA and GQDs exhibited two distinct bands at around 209 and 220 nm corresponding to α-helical structure of BSA. Additionally, a positive band was observed near 195 nm due to the β-sheet structure. Furthermore, the positive magnitude of ellipticity observed in the range of 190–200 nm was severely affected when BR was added suggesting the modification of the β-sheet structure. From the Fig. 5, it can be observed that the band at 195 nm shifted towards negative direction upon addition of 10 µM of BR to the mixture of GQDs and BSA. On the other hand, the bands at 209 and 220 nm corresponding to the α-helix exhibited a shift towards 230 nm, indicating a substantial loss of the helical structure. The spectral changes as observed in both α-helix and β-sheet structures of BSA suggest that BR can interact strongly with BSA even when it was associated with GQDs. The α-helix content of BSA was determined using equations (3) and (4) [47]:
(3)
α
h e l i x
( % )
=
[ - M R E
222
- 4000 ]
33000 - 4000
× 100
(4)
MRE
222
=
θ
10 C L
N r
Here, MRE222 represents the mean residual ellipticity; θ, the ellipticity value of the samples; C, concentration of protein sample (mol/L); L, the sample path length in cm; and Nr indicates the number of amino acid residues in the sample (583 for BSA). The α-helical content of BSA bound with GQDs was determined as 36.7 % which decreased to 22.9 % and 16.5 % with addition of BR of 5 and 10 µM, respectively.

The ITC measurement of the interaction between GQDs and BR shows a negative change in entropy (−42.4 cal/mol/deg). The typical thermogram of interaction between the GQDs and BR has been shown in the Fig. S3. This suggests that the formation of the complex results in a more organized arrangement of molecules. The significant negative change in enthalpy (−15.6 kcal/mol) contributes to the negative change in free energy, indicating that the formation of the complex between GQDs and BR is spontaneous and driven by enthalpy. The binding constant for the binding was obtained as 6.8 × 104 M−1 which is about three order of magnitude lower than the binding constant for BSA and BR [25,39,48]. This justifies the fact that BSA has higher binding affinity to BR than that towards GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of binding of BR to GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Understanding the selectivity of the developed probe for sensing of BR is crucial, especially in the presence of potentially co-existing substances like various metal ions (Fe2+, Na+, etc.) and biomolecules (L-cysteine, uric acid, ascorbic acid, glucose).

We monitored the change in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs in the presence of same concentration (70 µM) of each of these substances (Fig. 6 ). This figure illustrates that the extent of quenching in fluorescence intensity of GQDs was significantly higher in the presence of BR compared to that with other metal ions and biomolecules. This extraordinarily high fluorescence quenching is attributed to relatively strong binding of GQDs with BR and the occurrence of FRET from the excited state of GQDs to the ground state of BR as discussed in the previous section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of GQDs in determining BR of serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the utility of GQDs as a probe for quantifying BR in serum samples, we employed a spiking technique. The quenching of the emission spectrum of GQDs in the presence of BR was fitted to the linear Stern − Volmer relation (eqn. (1)), yielding a correlation coefficient of 0.997 (Fig. S4). The concentrations of BR, both added and that present in the serum sample, were determined from the calibration curve and are presented in Table 1 .

The present study demonstrates that the addition of BR to GQDs leads to a significant quenching of the fluorescence emission of GQDs with concomitant increase in emission of BR and this may be attributed to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process operating between the two components. The high Stern-Volmer quenching constant, the decrease in the average lifetime of GQDs on addition of BR and FTIR spectral data illustrate interactions between the two components, in question. Circular dichroism revealed strong interaction of BSA with BR even when associated with GQDs. It is established through isothermal calorimetric measurements that interaction between GQDs and BR is enthalpy driven process and the binding constant for these components determined as 6.8x104 M−1 is three order lower than that for BSA with BR. Thus, the observed results show that BSA may dissociate BR bound with GQDs. This has prompted us to observe that the subsequent addition of BSA to the mixture of GQDs and BR results in the recovery of the quenched emission intensity of GQDs, indicating an improvement in the structure of GQDs. It is also demonstrated that GQDs are highly selective towards BR among possible interfering substances. The observed results suggest that GQDs can be employed in detecting bilirubin at the level as low as 0.15 nM and it may also provide a platform to act as ‘switch-off-on’ sensor through incorporation of BSA. Further, it is observed that GQDs are quite stable in real serum sample and can detect BR very effectively.

Sudip Karmakar: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tushar Kanti Das: Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Abhijit Saha: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: S.K. would like to thank Department of Science & Technology, Govt. of India, for the INSPIRE Fellowship. T.K.D. is grateful to Council of Scientific and Industrial Research for the NET fellowship. The authors wish to acknowledge Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, Kolkata for providing with the CD measurement facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The influence of edge structure on the electronic properties of graphene quantum dots and nanoribbons
Nat. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A γ-cyclodextrin-based metal–organic framework embedded with graphene quantum dots and modified with PEGMA via SI-ATRP for anticancer drug delivery and therapy
